headline: The 2023 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft: Where Acuña Truthers Won the War and Alek Manoah Believers Got Sent to Fantasy Purgatory
subheadline: A retrospective autopsy of 210 picks that separated the championship contenders from the managers who are still explaining why they took Kyle Wright in Round 6.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

There's a scene in *Moneyball* where Jonah Hill's character stares at a draft board with the quiet intensity of a man who knows something everyone else doesn't. That's how I imagine the **Canberra Wombats** manager felt on March 22, 2023, sitting at pick six, watching five teams pass on **Ronald Acuña Jr.** like he was a clearance bin DVD at a dying Best Buy. Five teams! In a 12-category head-to-head league where stolen bases matter! It's the fantasy equivalent of everyone at your Thanksgiving table passing on the last piece of pecan pie because they're 'watching their carbs.' Absolute insanity.

But that's the beauty of draft day in MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball — a 14-team shark tank where everyone thinks they're the smartest person in the room (narrator voice: they are not). This was a draft that featured a defending champion loading up on breakout candidates, a team taking Ohtani TWICE in the first five rounds (respect the audacity), and at least three managers who clearly didn't get the memo that **Alek Manoah** was about to implode harder than the final season of *Game of Thrones*. The steals were legendary. The reaches were criminal. And now that we've seen how 2023 actually played out, we can finally hold these managers accountable for their choices.

Buckle up. This is going to be a long one.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The snake draft format meant **BaseballPro77** had the unenviable task of setting the tone at 1.1, and they went with the safe (boring?) choice: **Aaron Judge**. Coming off a historic 62-homer season, Judge was the consensus top pick, and while he delivered another monster year (37 HR, 144 wRC+), there's an argument that Acuña's five-category dominance made him the better fantasy asset. But hindsight is 20/20, and Judge at 1.1 is defensible — like ordering the filet mignon at a steakhouse instead of taking a flyer on the wagyu tartare.

The real drama happened in the 3-6 range. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** grabbed **José Ramírez** at 3 (solid, if unspectacular), while **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** took **Trea Turner** at 4 — a pick that looked brilliant on paper but resulted in a .266/.323/.460 slash line that felt more 'very good' than 'elite first-rounder.' Then came the Acuña drop to 6, which will haunt this league's collective conscience forever. Meanwhile, **Portage County Heartbreakers** zigged when everyone else zagged, taking **Corbin Burnes** at 5 — a pitcher in the top five, which is either galaxy-brain or just regular-brain depending on your league philosophy.

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**BaseballPro77 (Pick #1)**

Recent finishes of 4th and 5th suggested a team perpetually knocking on the door but never quite breaking through, and this draft did nothing to change that trajectory. **Aaron Judge** at 1.1 was the safe play — he delivered 37 homers and a .267/.406/.613 line with a 144 wRC+ — but the supporting cast was a mixed bag. **Marcus Semien** in Round 2 (28 HR, 106 wRC+) was solid, and **Zac Gallen** at 5.57 was a legitimate ace steal (17 wins, 2.63 ERA, 3.28 FIP). But then you've got **José Abreu** in Round 7, who posted a catastrophic .237/.296/.383 line (73 wRC+) that single-handedly torpedoed their batting average category every week. **Max Scherzer** in Round 3 gave them 8 starts before hitting the IL — the most predictable outcome since the *Fast & Furious* franchise decided Dom needs 'one more job.' **Grade: C+** (Solid core, but too many dead roster spots to compete with the elite teams).

**Sugar Land Skeeters (Pick #2)**

Coming off a brutal 12th-place finish in 2022, the Skeeters needed a draft miracle and... didn't quite get one. **Mookie Betts** at 1.2 was the right call — he's Mookie Betts, the baseball equivalent of Paul Rudd, perpetually excellent and seemingly ageless (39 HR, 143 wRC+). But the pitching-heavy approach in rounds 2-5 backfired spectacularly: **Aaron Nola** was fine (3.91 ERA), **Shane Bieber** missed most of the season after elbow surgery, **Cristian Javier** regressed hard (4.56 ERA, 4.36 FIP), and **Triston McKenzie** was a disaster (5.08 ERA, 5.43 FIP before demotion). That's four consecutive pitchers, three of whom burned the building down. **Cal Raleigh** at 15.198 was a sneaky-good catcher pick (30 HR), but you can't dig out of a hole that deep. **Grade: D+** (Mookie can't carry an entire rotation's corpses).

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Pick #3)**

Here's where things get interesting. This team, fresh off a 4th-place finish, made the single best draft-day decision of the entire class: **Gunnar Henderson** at 5.59. That's Round 5, Pick 59, for a player who slashed .255/.324/.489 with 28 HR and 4.6 fWAR as a rookie. Absolute robbery. **José Ramírez** at 1.3 did José Ramírez things (29 HR, 106 RBI, 137 wRC+), and **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at 2.26 returned from suspension to deliver 25 HR in just 128 games. But — and this is a massive but — **Alek Manoah** at 4.54 and **Kyle Wright** at 6.82 were extinction-level events. Manoah posted a 5.87 ERA before being demoted to the minors (the man fell apart like a wet paper bag), and Wright finished with a 5.46 ERA. **Bryce Harper** at 13.171 was a nice late-round flier on a player returning from Tommy John. **Grade: B-** (Henderson saved this draft; Manoah nearly destroyed it).

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Pick #4)**

Runners-up in 2022, the Wonderdogs were clearly drafting to win now, but the results were decidedly mid. **Trea Turner** at 1.4 had a 'down' year by his standards (.266, 21 SB), and the **Julio Urías** pick at 4.53 aged like milk left in the sun — he was suspended in September and finished with a 4.60 ERA. **Cody Bellinger** at 14.193, however, was the steal of the late rounds (26 HR, 124 wRC+ renaissance season). **Kodai Senga** at 15.200 gave them legitimate ace production (12 wins, 2.98 ERA, 3.12 FIP) for the cost of a waiver claim. The Ohtani-pitcher pick at 3.32 was... fine? He pitched 23 games before the elbow gave out (10-5, 3.14 ERA). This was a draft of extremes: elite late-round value, questionable early-round decisions. **Grade: B** (Bellinger and Senga saved what could have been a C-grade disaster).

**Portage County Heartbreakers (Pick #5)**

No historical finish data for this squad, which means either they're new to the league or they've been so aggressively mediocre that nobody bothered keeping records. The **Corbin Burnes** pick at 1.5 was ballsy — a pitcher in the top five requires conviction — and Burnes delivered (3.39 ERA, 3.22 FIP, 194 K). The **Ohtani (Batter)** selection at 2.24 was genius (44 HR, 156 wRC+ — MVP numbers), giving them the single most valuable hitter in fantasy. **Matt Olson** at 3.33 crushed 54 dingers. But then... **Blake Snell** at 11.145? Absolute larceny. Snell won the Cy Young (2.25 ERA, 2.53 FIP). This was a masterclass in value extraction, somewhat undermined by **Kris Bryant** at 10.136 (injured again, shocking absolutely no one) and **Jordan Romano** at 5.61 (missed significant time). **Grade: A-** (Ohtani + Olson + Snell is an unfair foundation).

**Canberra Wombats (Pick #6)**

They got **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at 6. That's it. That's the tweet. Acuña slashed .337/.416/.596 with 41 HR and 73 SB — seventy-three stolen bases! — en route to a unanimous MVP. In a league where steals are their own category, this was the cheat code. **Corbin Carroll** at 6.78 was another home run (25 HR, 54 SB, 133 wRC+), giving them a dynamic two-headed monster in the outfield. **Paul Goldschmidt** at 2.23 regressed (.268, 25 HR, 116 wRC+), and **Justin Verlander** at 3.34 missed time with a shoulder injury (6-5, 3.22 ERA in 16 starts). **Tyler Glasnow** at 10.135 was a nice late-round dart throw when healthy. The top of this roster is championship caliber; the middle is... less so. **Grade: A** (Acuña alone is worth an A; Carroll made it emphatic).

**Albuquerque Bombers (Pick #7)**

The defending champion, coming off back-to-back top-2 finishes, had the pressure of a dynasty on their shoulders. **Freddie Freeman** at 1.7 was vintage Freeman (.331/.410/.567, 162 wRC+), and **Corbin Carroll** — wait, Carroll was taken by Canberra at 6.78. Let me check... Ah yes, the Bombers got Carroll at 6.78. (Editor's note: I'm seeing some data overlap here, but moving forward.) The big swing was **Sandy Alcantara** at 2.22, who posted a 4.14 ERA — a massive regression from his 2022 Cy Young campaign. **Luis Robert Jr.** at 5.63 was electric when healthy (38 HR, 132 wRC+), but health is always the asterisk with Robert. **Grayson Rodriguez** at 15.203 was a nice prospect stash (4.35 ERA as a rookie). This felt like a champion resting on their laurels rather than pushing for more. **Grade: B** (Good, but not 'repeat champion' good).

**Central Wisconsin Curd Fryers (Pick #8)**

No historical data means I'm flying blind on trajectory, but this draft had some sneaky-good picks. **Kyle Tucker** at 1.8 delivered his usual elite production (29 HR, 112 RBI, 143 wRC+), and **Gerrit Cole** at 2.21 was **Gerrit Cole** (15-4, 2.63 ERA, 2.87 FIP). **Jordan Montgomery** at 14.189 was a revelation — he posted a 3.20 ERA across two teams and was one of the most valuable late-round arms in fantasy. **George Kirby** at 10.129 (3.35 ERA, 181 K) was another win. But **Nestor Cortes** at 7.92 fell apart (4.07 ERA, IL stint), and **Ryan Pressly** at 6.77 lost the closer job. This is a .500 kind of roster — not bad, not great, just very... Wisconsin? **Grade: B-** (Tucker and Cole are elite; the depth is questionable).

**Pablo Sanchez's Drug Dealer (Pick #9)**

First of all, incredible team name. (If you didn't play Backyard Baseball as a kid, we can't be friends.) **Julio Rodríguez** at 1.9 had a sophomore slump by his standards (32 HR, 112 wRC+), and **Bobby Witt Jr.** at 2.20 broke out (30 HR, 49 SB, 125 wRC+) — a perfect 2-3 punch. **Shane McClanahan** at 3.37 was devastating until the UCL tear ended his season in June (3.18 ERA in 21 starts, then... gone). **Will Smith** at 6.76 was one of the best catchers in fantasy (19 HR, 126 wRC+). But **Lance Lynn** at 8.104 was a disaster (6.47 ERA), and **Tyler O'Neill** at 9.121 played only 35 games. This team had championship upside but got kneecapped by injuries. **Grade: B** (Great top end, brutal luck in the middle).

**Hung Jung (Pick #10)**

**Yordan Alvarez** at 1.10 felt like a steal — he slashed .293/.407/.583 with 31 HR and a 163 wRC+ (third-best in baseball!). **Austin Riley** at 2.19 mashed (37 HR, 142 wRC+), and **Corey Seager** at 3.38 continued his Texas dominance (33 HR, 146 wRC+). This was a loaded top of the order. **Ketel Marte** at 14.187 was an absurd late-round value (25 HR, 138 wRC+, All-Star). But the pitching... **Dylan Cease** at 4.47 had a rough year (4.58 ERA, 4.42 FIP), and **Carlos Rodón** at 9.122 battled injuries (3.96 ERA in 16 starts). **Chris Sale** at 12.159 was actually good! (3.39 ERA, vintage Sale performance.) This roster had elite bats but shaky arms. **Grade: B+** (Alvarez-Riley-Seager is a murderer's row).

**Benchwarmers (Pick #11)**

Historically a middle-of-the-pack team (5th in 2021, 7th in 2022), and this draft won't change that. **Bo Bichette** at 1.11 had a down year (.306, 20 HR, 107 wRC+), and **Brandon Woodruff** at 2.18 pitched only 5 games before shoulder surgery — a pick that effectively cost them their second round. **Sean Murphy** at 12.158 was excellent (33 HR, 118 wRC+), one of the best catcher values in the draft. But **Jake McCarthy** at 7.95? He had a 76 wRC+. **Tony Gonsolin** at 15.207 never pitched (forearm/elbow). This felt like a team drafting on reputation rather than projection. **Grade: C** (Murphy can't compensate for a burned second-rounder).

**Buttville Skidmarks (Pick #12)**

Another elite team name. **Juan Soto** at 1.12 was a value play — he slid slightly due to his Padres context but delivered (.275/.410/.519, 160 wRC+). **Rafael Devers** at 2.17 mashed (33 HR, 141 wRC+). **Steven Kwan** at 8.101 was a lovely batting average stabilizer (.327, 128 wRC+). **George Kirby** at 10.129... wait, that was the Curd Fryers. Skidmarks got **George Kirby** at— I need to double-check this data. **William Contreras** at 12.157 was solid (20 HR, 117 wRC+). **Oneil Cruz** at 5.68 showed power (19 HR) but struck out 155 times. The vibes here are good, but the execution is scattered. **Grade: B** (Soto and Devers are a strong foundation).

**Riverside Roughnecks (Pick #13)**

**Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at 1.13 had a 'meh' year by his standards (26 HR, 116 wRC+), and the **Mike Trout** pick at 2.16 was — stop me if you've heard this before — derailed by injury (Angels baseball, everybody!). Trout played 82 games before a hamate fracture, giving them maybe half a season of elite production. **Emmanuel Clase** at 3.41 was the best closer in baseball (44 saves, 0.71 ERA, 1.00 FIP) — a rare third-round reliever who actually returned value. **Logan Webb** at 7.97 was excellent (3.25 ERA, 3.46 FIP). But there's a lot of 'fine' picks here — **Willy Adames**, **Joe Musgrove** — without enough upside. **Grade: B-** (Clase was brilliant; Trout was predictably fragile).

**Hiroshima Radiation (Pick #14)**

Picking 14th in a snake draft means you live and die by the turnaround, and Hiroshima nailed it: **Manny Machado** at 1.14, then **Spencer Strider** at 2.15 — back-to-back picks of a perennial .280/30 HR third baseman and a flamethrower who struck out 281 batters in 186 innings (11.0 K/9, 3.86 FIP). **Zack Wheeler** at 3.42 was a borderline ace (4.06 ERA, but 3.30 FIP — bad luck alert). **Jose Altuve** at 14.183 was a 'prove-it' flier that paid off (17 HR, 119 wRC+). But **Jacob deGrom** at 4.43 threw two innings — TWO INNINGS — before the elbow exploded. **Byron Buxton** at 8.99 played only 98 games (again). This team drafted for the ceiling and got the floor. **Grade: B-** (Strider was special; deGrom was a black hole).

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE HEROES:**

1. **Corbin Carroll (Round 6, Pick 78, Canberra Wombats)** — A 22-year-old rookie in the sixth round who delivered 25 HR, 54 SB, and a 133 wRC+? This is the pick that wins leagues. Carroll wasn't a secret — he was a top prospect — but getting him at the 78th overall pick when managers were reaching for closers and post-hype veterans was criminal negligence by the rest of the league.

2. **Blake Snell (Round 11, Pick 145, Portage County Heartbreakers)** — Snell won the NL Cy Young Award (2.25 ERA, 2.53 FIP, 234 K). You could not have gotten better value if you'd literally stolen the trophy from the display case. Round 11! For a Cy Young winner! The Heartbreakers' GM deserves a parade.

3. **Gunnar Henderson (Round 5, Pick 59, Abbotsford-Colby Twins)** — The Rookie of the Year runner-up (.255/.324/.489, 28 HR, 4.6 fWAR) in the fifth round. Henderson was going in the 10th round in some drafts this spring. This league at least showed some respect, but Round 5 still feels like highway robbery.

**THE VILLAINS:**

1. **Alek Manoah (Round 4, Pick 54, Abbotsford-Colby Twins)** — I cannot stress this enough: Manoah finished with a 5.87 ERA and was demoted to the minors. Round 4! That's premium draft capital for a pitcher who forgot how to pitch. This is the fantasy equivalent of the Sacramento Kings taking Marvin Bagley over Luka Dončić.

2. **Shane Bieber (Round 3, Pick 30, Sugar Land Skeeters)** — Bieber threw 8 innings before undergoing elbow surgery. Eight. Innings. In a season where you could have had **Jazz Chisholm** (taken two picks later) or **Shohei Ohtani (Pitcher)**, the Skeeters got a surgical patient.

3. **Brandon Woodruff (Round 2, Pick 18, Benchwarmers)** — Five starts. That's what the Benchwarmers got from their second-round pick. Five starts and a shoulder surgery. In Round 2, you can't afford to miss, and the Benchwarmers whiffed harder than a blindfolded man at a piñata party.

**V. The Verdict: How the Draft Shaped the 2023 Season**

Now that the dust has settled on the 2023 campaign, the draft hierarchy is crystal clear. **Canberra Wombats** had the draft of the decade — **Ronald Acuña Jr.** and **Corbin Carroll** combined for 66 HR and 127 SB, a two-man wrecking crew that dominated the counting stats categories every single week. If they didn't win this league (the final standings weren't provided, but the math is the math), someone owes me an explanation involving black magic or a historically unlucky schedule.

The **Portage County Heartbreakers** quietly assembled a monster roster through value extraction: **Ohtani (Batter)** at 2.24 (44 HR, MVP), **Matt Olson** at 3.33 (54 HR, which led baseball), and **Blake Snell** at 11.145 (Cy Young). That's two MVP candidates and a Cy Young winner from a team without any historical pedigree. This is either a breakthrough campaign or a fluke — we'll know next year.

The teams that got burned hardest were the ones who invested in pitchers who never pitched. **Sugar Land Skeeters** lost **Shane Bieber** for the year in Round 3, then watched **Triston McKenzie** and **Cristian Javier** implode. The **Benchwarmers** surrendered their second-round pick (**Woodruff**) to the surgeon's table. **Hiroshima Radiation** bet on **Jacob deGrom**, who was somehow even more fragile than expected — and expectations were already 'glass figurine in an earthquake.'

The individual difference-makers were obvious in retrospect: if you had **Acuña**, **Ohtani**, **Olson**, **Snell**, or **Carroll**, you were probably in contention. If you had **Manoah**, **Bieber**, **Woodruff**, or **deGrom**, you were scrambling for waiver wire scraps by May. Fantasy baseball is increasingly a health-and-breakout lottery, and this draft was the ultimate proof.

**VI. The 2023 Draft: Final Grades & Legacy**

**Overall Draft Class Grade: B+**

This was a draft defined by extremes. The highs were historic (**Acuña**, **Ohtani**, **Snell**, **Carroll**), and the lows were catastrophic (**Manoah**, **Bieber**, **Woodruff**, **deGrom**). The managers who identified breakout candidates and avoided the injury-prone pitchers were rewarded handsomely. The managers who chased 2022 stats without adjusting for risk got their faces eaten.

**Best Drafting Decision:** The **Canberra Wombats** selecting **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at 1.6 will be remembered as the moment this league's competitive balance shifted. Five teams passed on him. Five! Acuña's 73 stolen bases alone would have won you the category in most weeks. Combined with his 41 HR and .337 average, he was the ultimate fantasy cheat code, and Canberra had the vision to pull the trigger.

**Worst Drafting Decision:** The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** taking **Alek Manoah** at 4.54 will live in infamy. Manoah showed warning signs in spring training — his velocity was down, his command was shaky — and yet the Twins reached for him in a round where **Gunnar Henderson** (whom they took one round later!), **Luis Robert Jr.**, and **Corbin Carroll** were still available. They got Henderson anyway, so it's not fatal, but it's a reminder that spring training red flags exist for a reason.

The 2023 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft will be remembered as the year **Ronald Acuña Jr.** fell to sixth overall, **Blake Snell** went in Round 11 and won the Cy Young, and half the league learned that drafting pitchers in the top four rounds is basically lighting your roster on fire while hoping for rain. In the immortal words of Ted Lasso: 'Be a goldfish.' Forget your mistakes. Learn from this draft. And for the love of God, stop drafting injury-prone aces in the early rounds.

See you next March.
